speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been really something to meet up with them again after all that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. And you see, we considered-- well they were our boss, [? Hershfield ?] and [? Beamish, ?] as we called him. And Jean [? Guilmelon. ?] These were the people who-- they were the brain trust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they much older than you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. A few years, a few years older. But he had been in the Spanish Civil War and he knew about this trip across the mountains. He was the one who discovered that way. And Jean [? Guilmelon ?] was, I think, something in the intelligence service before. He was very, very helpful. Very nice people. And of course they were older than I was, but they're still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- that's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then of course, we loved Mary Jayne and Miriam because they were the women around. And very dedicated. Mary Jayne was quite-- she was quite-- she was sort of a debutante type in Paris before, with her plane and she would cruise around in it. And she had a little money and she gave it all to the organization. And as I say, everybody-- we're all volunteers. We were behaving, you could see we weren't professionals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they sent to France by the same group, or was it just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only Varian. They were in Europe. He recruited them in Europe, one after the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were born in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Beamish ?] was from, from I think, Munich. And Jean [? Guilmelon ?] was from France. And one another was from Berlin, I think. And they had a secretary for a while, Lena Fishman, who was very good. She was really good. She flirted a little bit, I think, with this Captain [? DuBois ?] to get all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: He liked her a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did she come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany, also. And-- but Mary Jayne and Miriam were Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how come they were in the country at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Students.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were also students. Well Mary Jayne was just having a hell of a good time. Did you tell [? Michelle ?] about-- are you shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, come here. She can see it, can't she?

speaker: interviewer
text: I've just got one more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Is there anything that you would like to leave in the way of a message or a statement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well let's say, when they say that this is never going to happen again, they better be damn sure that it doesn't. Because let me tell you, if they don't-- if they aren't careful this is the sort of thing that could happen again. In fact there are some places like Bosnia and all now, and it's waiting to happen. But this question of anti-Semitic feelings and all of that has got to-- this has got to be confronted, you know what I mean? It's just a lot of things that-- that-- that should have taught us, and I wonder if it really did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we better think awfully careful about where we're heading today, you know? Because-- you know, racial questions-- there's no place for all of that anymore. And I think that that should have taught us, and I don't know if it did. You never know. You never know. People-- people don't talk with me very much, because they know how I feel so. But you know, I just hope it will never happen again, anything like that. Thank you. Would you like to introduce your wife, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we met in Rome. This is my wife April Mary Duxbury Fawcett. And finally we have on our bell downstairs her name Fawcett. And she is the inspiration of everything for me right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's really nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I say, everybody has to have a big passion in life, so I have my wife and the thing in Marseille.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. I understand that you didn't know for a long time about Charles's work in Marseille? Or he didn't talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he never talked about it to anybody. Only he gradually, over 36 years being with him a lot, it gradually seeped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you're quite amazed when you first heard about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was amazed, but then on the other hand I wasn't because before I even met him I'd heard that he was always involved in causes and that he'd done all sorts of things that people vaguely knew about-- the Congo and the Hungarian and various things. And so it was just part of Charlie, you know, part of the sort of things he would normally do. He always wanted to help people, even though sometimes it was a minute thing like lending them the bus fare or helping them go and see their-- their grandmother in America. He just all the time wanted to help. And-- especially, of course, if they were in mortal danger. Then he really did. So no, it wasn't a surprise at all. And I knew we had Marseille in common. That was the weird thing, that Charlie had never met anyone else who liked Marseille. And I was an au-pair girl there in the middle 50s. And I adored the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a very interesting experience there once. Just when she started with this family, because she's like I am, she likes to travel. And the family went away on vacation and left her in charge of the three children-- one, a very little baby. They had just met her practically. And when they came back the children were healthier and they loved her--

speaker: interviewee
text: And on an English diet. [LAUGHTER] Off the French diet immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Marseille.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you must have been quite young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I adored it. I really enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Marseille.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was something you mentioned just before we started rolling again that you had forgotten that is important from your story. So, you know, would you like to add that now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just about this marvelous mayor. What was the name? April is my memory, by the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Gaston Defferre, who was after--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was our lawyer. He took lots of chances, and he became-- how many years was he mayor?

speaker: interviewee
text: 45 Years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards. After the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Famous, famous mayor of Marseille. And died just recently. And I'm afraid he died before the Holocaust Museum, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he died just before that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he would have been very-- he was greatly appreciated by everyone, because he was the one who represented Fry and everybody when they had to go to-- he took lots of chances. Lots of chances.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say he represented Fry and others when they went to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He advised them legally what to do. Not that-- not that--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he also risked his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Time and time again.

speaker: interviewee
text: You weren't popular if you helped these people. He had to-- they had to go to the-- where? The prefecture, or places like that, didn't they? So a solicitor going with them helped them, because he would speak for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interpret, also. He would interpret-- I remember Fry told me, he said I would say so and so and all of a sudden-- Fry understood very well what he was saying, but he pretended he didn't. He said I would hear something quite different from what [LAUGHS] I'd said, but he was putting it in a way that was, you know, looked-- made it look better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, more of a legal language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or more what they should have answered.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they should have answered. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He knew what they should have gone and said.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a-- he was very young, see? And he became a great personality, almost as popular as the football players there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he become the mayor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon after that, didn't he, April?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well he must have done, because he died in about 1990. And he had been mayor for 45 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. So once he got that job he kept it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yes. He kept it. So he obviously had great tenacity.

speaker: interviewee
text: And became-- and became very famous.

speaker: interviewee
text: He put Marseille on the map. So he wasn't a man who could be frightened of things, you know? Because Marseille was always a very frightening and tough place. So if he survived that, he could survive anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a motor home, quite a luxurious motor home at one time, and we used to travel between Rome and Morocco and different places. I used it a lot in my work. And April used to come and go with me and some of my trips. And we went through Marseille one of those times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little narrow streets weren't conducive [LAUGHTER] [INTERPOSING VOICES] to our motor home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went and saw this gentleman, the mayor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was the point. We didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We got there six months after he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so sorry, because we would have loved to have seen him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You missed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, see I hadn't really given it any thought, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, so many other things had happened during the years that this was just something that he was involved with a long time ago when he was very young. So he forgot about it until the Holocaust things started happening, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were called, or invited, to go along--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Washington, yes. For the award to Varian. And then they told us about the museum, and what was going to happen in about two years time. And then we went when the little museum opened. The big museum opened, and then about six months later his exhibit, the one with Varian, opened in the basement. So that's when we went a second time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had-- also at one time, there was a program in the rotunda. In the Capitol, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but that was when Varian was awarded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was after his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. He died in '67, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were-- And there were a couple of my ancestors on the friezes around the-- around the building and-- We had a good time in Washington, didn't we?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love this-- One of them, Susan Morgenstern, became a great friend of ours. We had some nice experiences with the Holocaust Museum. There were-- then we saw three or four people. We saw Mary Jayne and Miriam, Davenport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one man who had been a little boy near the frontier where [? Fithco ?] was, you know I told you the man who used to have the wine thing. He had-- his family had sent him away to get him out of danger, because the family was working in the underground with us. And he was writing-- they exchanged letters, and one of the letters he wrote exposed it to the Gestapo and they caught his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So April and I met this man in Washington, and he started telling me the story. And he had tears rolling down his cheek and all. I said to him, look, you were how old? Don't you ever tell that story again. Don't you ever even think about it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he obviously had, for 45 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had. It just ruined him.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing was, there were all these little villages on the border. And the villagers used to hide the refugees just before they'd be taken over the mountains. And-- because then they'd been nearby. And so a lot of the children were sent away, because it had been going on for some time and they felt that eventually the German-- the Vichy police and the Germans and everyone would find out about it. And his father was not only the mayor of that village, but he was organizing all the villages in this thing. And the Holocaust Museum organized an exhibit, also in the basement, for these villagers to honor them. And of course, none of the original mayors or people were alive, but some of the children came back. And this one, he's now mayor of that village again, was particularly distraught because of the memories of what happened. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was so sad to see him so upset by it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the extraordinary thing was that in those villages, everybody knows everybody's business. So everybody knew when there was a stranger in town. So all these people hidden, they must have stuck out like sore thumbs. But they-- nobody said a word. They just suspected, they began to suspect, and that's why they were opening the mail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Se how, some of the French-- I wish this came out more, because a lot of the French were very helpful. And of course, there were collaborators too, but I mean, an awful lot of them were very helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. But then, you say about this boy, now a man, being so upset. People have been so affected by what happened for so many years in such huge numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. And I think it was sort of buried for a long time. People couldn't bear to think about it. And it was better not to think about it. But I think it's now, people are able to think about it and talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well maybe because of the age they've reached, they have more time on their hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Well they know that they may not be around for long, so they think God, if we don't talk about it now, this is just going to fade away as if it never happened. And then they see it happening in a similar way, not as large, in other places. So they probably think we better talk quickly. We better.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the more they do, the better it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we say, it has got to not happen again.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it does, all the time. I mean look at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Bosnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you. I must say we enjoyed it. You see, I did pretty well. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this in uniform, that's me when I was in the Ambulance Corp when I first started working for Fry. I kept it on because first, it's all the clothes I had, and second my first job was sort of trying to keep order and the people coming into the office he was using as a front for the operation. The lady with me there helped us a little bit, because she was living with the consul-- the Mexican consul there. This is Cassis, a little port outside of Marseille. And they were quite helpful to us. We hid some of our people on their boats from time to time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was just after the war. After the war in France, I mean. I was thinking the war-- it was just after the invasion of France. And these all were people who we just knew, it was sort of a cover for-- this little village was a place where we quite frequently hid people. And all of these people were in one way or another indirectly helping us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this one, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was an interview with the BBC for the-- when I was in the Royal Air Force. And I think they talked a little bit about the soldiers that we'd brought out of Paris to Marseille. And it wasn't-- this was a long time after the event.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this first photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was with Jean-Pierre Aumont. The first film, when the hat that the captain was to wear fitted me and so they chose me for the role. Just to stand in, and if then when they got back to Paris they would get a real actor. And they seemed to like what I did, so that was the start of my film career, where I made about 90 or so films all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a rule I played the villain in films, but in this case I was the father of the little girl who was-- who saw her mother murdered and went blind. It was a very famous film and a story book in Italy that they made the film of. And in the film I play the father of the girl. And it was the leading role because-- they chose me because with that hairpiece on I could play, this is when he was younger, and then without the hairpiece and my usual lines and everything I could play him much older. When he was a decrepit old wreck much later. So they chose me for the lead in the film on that. It was called Rental.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's with Dennis O'Keefe. I was the villain. And we have a fight in the end of the film, and it's rather spectacular. And he was a marvelous person to work with. I got to know so many of these American actors, because in Europe I was one of the few Americans who was already over here. And that was my main reason for getting these roles, I suppose. But I had a lot of good roles as a villain. I love playing villains. Maybe it's my true nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this picture please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was a French, Italian film that I had the lead in it with Sophia Loren. She was a wonderful person to work with. She was a lot of fun and always kept everybody in a great mood, and a marvelous actress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about these?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are just paintings of-- from memory, most of them, from around the world. [BACKGROUND NOISE] People that I've known around the world. And I gave them to a friend of mine a long time ago and he photographed these and sent them to me. Of course, what I really studied was sculpture, but I like painting too. Probably one day I'll end up trying-- trying to do it again.
